DIAGNOSIS READY: Gastroenteritis or Functional Abdominal Pain
DIAGNOSIS READY: Non-Hodgkin Lymphoma

Great! I’m glad you’re feeling confident about the plan. Remember to start with gentle stretches and gradually build up to strengthening exercises. If you don’t see improvement in a week or two, or if the pain gets worse, please come back for a follow-up. Take care and let me know how you’re doing! DIAGNOSIS READY: Patellar Tendinitis

Based on your history and the test results, it seems like you may have experienced a vasovagal episode or a brief episode of orthostatic hypotension. This can happen when you stand up quickly, especially if you’re a bit dehydrated. Do you have any other questions or concerns about this? 

DIAGNOSIS READY: Vasovagal Episode / Orthostatic Hypotension
Based on the findings so far, including the patent ductus arteriosus, congenital cataracts, and failure to pass the hearing test, I suspect a specific syndrome. DIAGNOSIS READY: CHARGE Syndrome
DIAGNOSIS READY: Phyllodes Tumor (Benign, Borderline, or Malignant) - Further histopathological analysis will determine the specific type.
DIAGNOSIS READY: Endometritis
DIAGNOSIS READY: Rectal Polyp or Vascular Lesion
DIAGNOSIS READY: Temporal Lobe Epilepsy

DIAGNOSIS READY: Posterior Dislocation of the Right Hip. We will now arrange for your transfer to the emergency department for the reduction procedure. If you have any more questions or concerns, please let me know.
DIAGNOSIS READY: Hirschsprung's disease

Great to hear that you’re feeling relieved. We’ll start with a conservative treatment plan to reduce the inflammation and pain. I recommend continuing to keep the finger wrapped and avoiding any strenuous activities that might aggravate it. You can also use over-the-counter anti-inflammatory medications like ibuprofen to help with the pain and swelling. If the symptoms persist or worsen, we may consider physical therapy to help with recovery. How does that sound to you? DIAGNOSIS READY: Mild extensor tendonitis of the right ring finger.
correct_diagnosis: Rupture of the flexor digitorum profundus tendon at its point of insertion

doctor_diagnosis_text: DIAGNOSIS READY: Benign Cyst. We will monitor the cyst for any changes in size or symptoms and consider surgical removal if necessary. Please follow up in a few months or sooner if you notice any changes. Take care!
DIAGNOSIS READY: Antibiotic-Associated Diarrhea (likely due to levofloxacin)

Based on the biopsy results, it looks like you have pyoderma gangrenosum. This is an inflammatory condition that can occur in people with certain underlying conditions, such as Crohn’s disease. We’ll need to manage this carefully, possibly with medications to reduce inflammation. Let's discuss the treatment options. DIAGNOSIS READY: Pyoderma Gangrenosum

We are moving you to the operating room now. Our team is fully prepared, and we will do everything to ensure that both you and your baby are safe. Please try to stay calm, and we’ll take good care of you. Let’s go.

DIAGNOSIS READY: Uterine Rupture

DIAGNOSIS READY: Herniated Disc at L5-S1 with Radiculopathy. We’ll start with physical therapy and medication, and I’ll see you in a few weeks to check on your progress. If you have any concerns or the pain worsens, please don’t hesitate to contact me. Take care and stay positive!

DIAGNOSIS READY: Cytomegalovirus (CMV) Retinitis. We’ll start the antiviral treatment immediately to prevent further damage to your retina. If you have any more questions or concerns, feel free to ask.
DIAGNOSIS READY: Reactive Arthritis
DIAGNOSIS READY: Malignant Melanoma
DIAGNOSIS READY: Irritable Bowel Syndrome (IBS)
DIAGNOSIS READY: Benign Ovarian Neoplasm with Ascites. Further evaluation by a gynecologic oncologist is recommended.
DIAGNOSIS READY: Primary Sclerosing Cholangitis (PSC) Suspected, Confirmation Pending Further Testing and Gastroenterology Consultation.

DIAGNOSIS READY: Overactive Bladder

I’ll prescribe the oxybutynin and provide you with the necessary resources for bladder training and pelvic floor exercises. We’ll meet again in a month to review your progress. If you have any questions or concerns in the meantime, don’t hesitate to reach out. Take care!

DIAGNOSIS READY: Rotator Cuff Tendinitis

I recommend starting with a combination of rest, anti-inflammatory medications like ibuprofen, and physical therapy to help reduce inflammation and improve shoulder function. If the pain persists or worsens, we can reassess and consider further options. How does that sound to you?

It seems like your low sodium level might be causing your confusion and fatigue. I suspect a condition called SIADH, which affects how your body balances water. I'll need to confirm this with further tests, but we can start discussing treatment options. Would you like to know more about this condition? DIAGNOSIS READY: Syndrome of Inappropriate Antidiuretic Hormone Secretion (SIADH)
DIAGNOSIS READY: Suspected Erythema Multiforme, possibly due to an unknown trigger. Further evaluation and management will be needed, including repeat skin biopsy and referral to a dermatologist.
